short_excerpt: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others ¶
text: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others ¶

short_excerpt: By default, FastAPI will return JSON responses.
text: By default, FastAPI will return JSON responses.

short_excerpt: You can override it by returning a Response directly as seen in Return a Response directly .
text: You can override it by returning a Response directly as seen in Return a Response directly .

short_excerpt: But if you return a Response directly (or any subclass, like JSONResponse ), the data won't be automatically converted (even if you declare a response_model ), and the documentation won't be automatically generated (for example, including the specific "media type", in the HTTP header Content-Type as part of the generated OpenAPI).
text: But if you return a Response directly (or any subclass, like JSONResponse ), the data won't be automatically converted (even if you declare a response_model ), and the documentation won't be automatically generated (for example, including the specific "media type", in the HTTP header Content-Type as part of the generated OpenAPI).

short_excerpt: But you can also declare the Response that you want to be used (e.g. any Response subclass), in the path operation decorator using the response_class parameter.
text: But you can also declare the Response that you want to be used (e.g. any Response subclass), in the path operation decorator using the response_class parameter.

short_excerpt: The contents that you return from your path operation function will be put inside of that Response .
text: The contents that you return from your path operation function will be put inside of that Response .

short_excerpt: Note
text: Note

short_excerpt: If you use a response class with no media type, FastAPI will expect your response to have no content, so it will not document the response format in its generated OpenAPI docs.
text: If you use a response class with no media type, FastAPI will expect your response to have no content, so it will not document the response format in its generated OpenAPI docs.

short_excerpt: JSON Responses ¶
text: JSON Responses ¶

short_excerpt: By default FastAPI returns JSON responses.
text: By default FastAPI returns JSON responses.

short_excerpt: If you declare a Response Model FastAPI will use it to serialize the data to JSON, using Pydantic.
text: If you declare a Response Model FastAPI will use it to serialize the data to JSON, using Pydantic.

short_excerpt: If you don't declare a response model, FastAPI will use the jsonable_encoder explained in JSON Compatible Encoder and put it in a JSONResponse .
text: If you don't declare a response model, FastAPI will use the jsonable_encoder explained in JSON Compatible Encoder and put it in a JSONResponse .

short_excerpt: If you declare a response_class with a JSON media type ( application/json ), like is the case with the JSONResponse , the data you return will be automatically converted (and filtered) with any Pydantic response_model that you declared in the path operation decorator . But the data won't be serialized to JSON bytes with Pydantic, instead it will be converted with the jsonable_encoder and then passed to the JSONResponse class, which will serialize it to bytes using the standard JSON library in Python.
text: If you declare a response_class with a JSON media type ( application/json ), like is the case with the JSONResponse , the data you return will be automatically converted (and filtered) with any Pydantic response_model that you declared in the path operation decorator . But the data won't be serialized to JSON bytes with Pydantic, instead it will be converted with the jsonable_encoder and then passed to the JSONResponse class, which will serialize it to bytes using the standard JSON library in Python.

short_excerpt: JSON Performance ¶
text: JSON Performance ¶

short_excerpt: In short, if you want the maximum performance, use a Response Model and don't declare a response_class in the path operation decorator .
text: In short, if you want the maximum performance, use a Response Model and don't declare a response_class in the path operation decorator .

short_excerpt: # Code above omitted 👆 @app . post ( "/items/" ) async def create_item ( item : Item ) -> Item : return item # Code below omitted 👇
text: # Code above omitted 👆 @app . post ( "/items/" ) async def create_item ( item : Item ) -> Item : return item # Code below omitted 👇

short_excerpt: from fastapi import FastAPI from pydantic import BaseModel app = FastAPI () class Item ( BaseModel ): name : str description : str | None = None price : float tax : float | None = None tags : list [ str ] = [] @app . post ( "/items/" ) async def create_item ( item : Item ) -> Item : return item @app . get ( "/items/" ) async def read_items () -> list [ Item ]: return [ Item ( name = "Portal Gun" , price = 42.0 ), Item ( name = "Plumbus" , price = 32.0 ), ]
text: from fastapi import FastAPI from pydantic import BaseModel app = FastAPI () class Item ( BaseModel ): name : str description : str | None = None price : float tax : float | None = None tags : list [ str ] = [] @app . post ( "/items/" ) async def create_item ( item : Item ) -> Item : return item @app . get ( "/items/" ) async def read_items () -> list [ Item ]: return [ Item ( name = "Portal Gun" , price = 42.0 ), Item ( name = "Plumbus" , price = 32.0 ), ]

short_excerpt: HTML Response ¶
text: HTML Response ¶

short_excerpt: To return a response with HTML directly from FastAPI , use HTMLResponse .
text: To return a response with HTML directly from FastAPI , use HTMLResponse .

short_excerpt: Import HTMLResponse .
text: Import HTMLResponse .

short_excerpt: Pass HTMLResponse as the parameter response_class of your path operation decorator .
text: Pass HTMLResponse as the parameter response_class of your path operation decorator .

short_excerpt:
from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/items/" , response_class = HTMLResponse ) async def read_items (): return """ Some HTML in here

Look ma! HTML!

"""
text:
from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/items/" , response_class = HTMLResponse ) async def read_items (): return """ Some HTML in here

Look ma! HTML!

"""

short_excerpt: The parameter response_class will also be used to define the "media type" of the response.
text: The parameter response_class will also be used to define the "media type" of the response.

short_excerpt: In this case, the HTTP header Content-Type will be set to text/html .
text: In this case, the HTTP header Content-Type will be set to text/html .

short_excerpt: And it will be documented as such in OpenAPI.
text: And it will be documented as such in OpenAPI.

short_excerpt: Return a Response ¶
text: Return a Response ¶

short_excerpt: As seen in Return a Response directly , you can also override the response directly in your path operation , by returning it.
text: As seen in Return a Response directly , you can also override the response directly in your path operation , by returning it.

short_excerpt: The same example from above, returning an HTMLResponse , could look like:
text: The same example from above, returning an HTMLResponse , could look like:

short_excerpt:
from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/items/" ) async def read_items (): html_content = """ Some HTML in here

Look ma! HTML!

""" return HTMLResponse ( content = html_content , status_code = 200 )
text:
from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/items/" ) async def read_items (): html_content = """ Some HTML in here

Look ma! HTML!

""" return HTMLResponse ( content = html_content , status_code = 200 )

short_excerpt: Warning
text: Warning

short_excerpt: A Response returned directly by your path operation function won't be documented in OpenAPI (for example, the Content-Type won't be documented) and won't be visible in the automatic interactive docs.
text: A Response returned directly by your path operation function won't be documented in OpenAPI (for example, the Content-Type won't be documented) and won't be visible in the automatic interactive docs.

short_excerpt: Of course, the actual Content-Type header, status code, etc, will come from the Response object you returned.
text: Of course, the actual Content-Type header, status code, etc, will come from the Response object you returned.

short_excerpt: Document in OpenAPI and override Response ¶
text: Document in OpenAPI and override Response ¶

short_excerpt: If you want to override the response from inside of the function but at the same time document the "media type" in OpenAPI, you can use the response_class parameter AND return a Response object.
text: If you want to override the response from inside of the function but at the same time document the "media type" in OpenAPI, you can use the response_class parameter AND return a Response object.

short_excerpt: The response_class will then be used only to document the OpenAPI path operation , but your Response will be used as is.
text: The response_class will then be used only to document the OpenAPI path operation , but your Response will be used as is.

short_excerpt: HTMLResponse
text: HTMLResponse

short_excerpt: For example, it could be something like:
text: For example, it could be something like:

short_excerpt:
from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI () def generate_html_response (): html_content = """ Some HTML in here

Look ma! HTML!

""" return HTMLResponse ( content = html_content , status_code = 200 ) @app . get ( "/items/" , response_class = HTMLResponse ) async def read_items (): return generate_html_response ()
text:
from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI () def generate_html_response (): html_content = """ Some HTML in here

Look ma! HTML!

""" return HTMLResponse ( content = html_content , status_code = 200 ) @app . get ( "/items/" , response_class = HTMLResponse ) async def read_items (): return generate_html_response ()

short_excerpt: In this example, the function generate_html_response() already generates and returns a Response instead of returning the HTML in a str .
text: In this example, the function generate_html_response() already generates and returns a Response instead of returning the HTML in a str .

short_excerpt: By returning the result of calling generate_html_response() , you are already returning a Response that will override the default FastAPI behavior.
text: By returning the result of calling generate_html_response() , you are already returning a Response that will override the default FastAPI behavior.

short_excerpt: But as you passed the HTMLResponse in the response_class too, FastAPI will know how to document it in OpenAPI and the interactive docs as HTML with text/html :
text: But as you passed the HTMLResponse in the response_class too, FastAPI will know how to document it in OpenAPI and the interactive docs as HTML with text/html :

short_excerpt: Available responses ¶
text: Available responses ¶

short_excerpt: Here are some of the available responses.
text: Here are some of the available responses.

short_excerpt: Keep in mind that you can use Response to return anything else, or even create a custom sub-class.
text: Keep in mind that you can use Response to return anything else, or even create a custom sub-class.

short_excerpt: Technical Details
text: Technical Details

short_excerpt: You could also use from starlette.responses import HTMLResponse .
text: You could also use from starlette.responses import HTMLResponse .

short_excerpt: FastAPI provides the same starlette.responses as fastapi.responses just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette.
text: FastAPI provides the same starlette.responses as fastapi.responses just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette.

short_excerpt: Response ¶
text: Response ¶

short_excerpt: The main Response class, all the other responses inherit from it.
text: The main Response class, all the other responses inherit from it.

short_excerpt: You can return it directly.
text: You can return it directly.

short_excerpt: It accepts the following parameters:
text: It accepts the following parameters:

short_excerpt: content - A str or bytes .
text: content - A str or bytes .

short_excerpt: status_code - An int HTTP status code.
text: status_code - An int HTTP status code.

short_excerpt: headers - A dict of strings.
text: headers - A dict of strings.

short_excerpt: media_type - A str giving the media type. E.g. "text/html" .
text: media_type - A str giving the media type. E.g. "text/html" .

short_excerpt: FastAPI (actually Starlette) will automatically include a Content-Length header. It will also include a Content-Type header, based on the media_type and appending a charset for text types.
text: FastAPI (actually Starlette) will automatically include a Content-Length header. It will also include a Content-Type header, based on the media_type and appending a charset for text types.

short_excerpt: from fastapi import FastAPI , Response app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/legacy/" ) def get_legacy_data (): data = """<?xml version="1.0"?> <shampoo> <Header> Apply shampoo here. </Header> <Body> You'll have to use soap here. </Body> </shampoo> """ return Response ( content = data , media_type = "application/xml" )
text: from fastapi import FastAPI , Response app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/legacy/" ) def get_legacy_data (): data = """<?xml version="1.0"?> <shampoo> <Header> Apply shampoo here. </Header> <Body> You'll have to use soap here. </Body> </shampoo> """ return Response ( content = data , media_type = "application/xml" )

short_excerpt: HTMLResponse ¶
text: HTMLResponse ¶

short_excerpt: Takes some text or bytes and returns an HTML response, as you read above.
text: Takes some text or bytes and returns an HTML response, as you read above.

short_excerpt: PlainTextResponse ¶
text: PlainTextResponse ¶

short_excerpt: Takes some text or bytes and returns a plain text response.
text: Takes some text or bytes and returns a plain text response.

short_excerpt: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import PlainTextResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/" , response_class = PlainTextResponse ) async def main (): return "Hello World"
text: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import PlainTextResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/" , response_class = PlainTextResponse ) async def main (): return "Hello World"

short_excerpt: JSONResponse ¶
text: JSONResponse ¶

short_excerpt: Takes some data and returns an application/json encoded response.
text: Takes some data and returns an application/json encoded response.

short_excerpt: This is the default response used in FastAPI , as you read above.
text: This is the default response used in FastAPI , as you read above.

short_excerpt: But if you declare a response model or return type, that will be used directly to serialize the data to JSON, and a response with the right media type for JSON will be returned directly, without using the JSONResponse class.
text: But if you declare a response model or return type, that will be used directly to serialize the data to JSON, and a response with the right media type for JSON will be returned directly, without using the JSONResponse class.

short_excerpt: This is the ideal way to get the best performance.
text: This is the ideal way to get the best performance.

short_excerpt: RedirectResponse ¶
text: RedirectResponse ¶

short_excerpt: Returns an HTTP redirect. Uses a 307 status code (Temporary Redirect) by default.
text: Returns an HTTP redirect. Uses a 307 status code (Temporary Redirect) by default.

short_excerpt: You can return a RedirectResponse directly:
text: You can return a RedirectResponse directly:

short_excerpt: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/typer" ) async def redirect_typer (): return RedirectResponse ( "https://typer.tiangolo.com" )
text: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/typer" ) async def redirect_typer (): return RedirectResponse ( "https://typer.tiangolo.com" )

short_excerpt: Or you can use it in the response_class parameter:
text: Or you can use it in the response_class parameter:

short_excerpt: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/fastapi" , response_class = RedirectResponse ) async def redirect_fastapi (): return "https://fastapi.tiangolo.com"
text: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/fastapi" , response_class = RedirectResponse ) async def redirect_fastapi (): return "https://fastapi.tiangolo.com"

short_excerpt: If you do that, then you can return the URL directly from your path operation function.
text: If you do that, then you can return the URL directly from your path operation function.

short_excerpt: In this case, the status_code used will be the default one for the RedirectResponse , which is 307 .
text: In this case, the status_code used will be the default one for the RedirectResponse , which is 307 .

short_excerpt: You can also use the status_code parameter combined with the response_class parameter:
text: You can also use the status_code parameter combined with the response_class parameter:

short_excerpt: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/pydantic" , response_class = RedirectResponse , status_code = 302 ) async def redirect_pydantic (): return "https://docs.pydantic.dev/"
text: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/pydantic" , response_class = RedirectResponse , status_code = 302 ) async def redirect_pydantic (): return "https://docs.pydantic.dev/"

short_excerpt: StreamingResponse ¶
text: StreamingResponse ¶

short_excerpt: Takes an async generator or a normal generator/iterator (a function with yield ) and streams the response body.
text: Takes an async generator or a normal generator/iterator (a function with yield ) and streams the response body.

short_excerpt: import anyio from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse app = FastAPI () async def fake_video_streamer (): for i in range ( 10 ): yield b "some fake video bytes" await anyio . sleep ( 0 ) @app . get ( "/" ) async def main (): return StreamingResponse ( fake_video_streamer ())
text: import anyio from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse app = FastAPI () async def fake_video_streamer (): for i in range ( 10 ): yield b "some fake video bytes" await anyio . sleep ( 0 ) @app . get ( "/" ) async def main (): return StreamingResponse ( fake_video_streamer ())

short_excerpt: An async task can only be cancelled when it reaches an await . If there is no await , the generator (function with yield ) can not be cancelled properly and may keep running even after cancellation is requested.
text: An async task can only be cancelled when it reaches an await . If there is no await , the generator (function with yield ) can not be cancelled properly and may keep running even after cancellation is requested.

short_excerpt: Since this small example does not need any await statements, we add an await anyio.sleep(0) to give the event loop a chance to handle cancellation.
text: Since this small example does not need any await statements, we add an await anyio.sleep(0) to give the event loop a chance to handle cancellation.

short_excerpt: This would be even more important with large or infinite streams.
text: This would be even more important with large or infinite streams.

short_excerpt: Tip
text: Tip

short_excerpt: Instead of returning a StreamingResponse directly, you should probably follow the style in Stream Data , it's much more convenient and handles cancellation behind the scenes for you.
text: Instead of returning a StreamingResponse directly, you should probably follow the style in Stream Data , it's much more convenient and handles cancellation behind the scenes for you.

short_excerpt: If you are streaming JSON Lines, follow the Stream JSON Lines tutorial.
text: If you are streaming JSON Lines, follow the Stream JSON Lines tutorial.

short_excerpt: FileResponse ¶
text: FileResponse ¶

short_excerpt: Asynchronously streams a file as the response.
text: Asynchronously streams a file as the response.

short_excerpt: Takes a different set of arguments to instantiate than the other response types:
text: Takes a different set of arguments to instantiate than the other response types:

short_excerpt: path - The file path to the file to stream.
text: path - The file path to the file to stream.

short_excerpt: headers - Any custom headers to include, as a dictionary.
text: headers - Any custom headers to include, as a dictionary.

short_excerpt: media_type - A string giving the media type. If unset, the filename or path will be used to infer a media type.
text: media_type - A string giving the media type. If unset, the filename or path will be used to infer a media type.

short_excerpt: filename - If set, this will be included in the response Content-Disposition .
text: filename - If set, this will be included in the response Content-Disposition .

short_excerpt: File responses will include appropriate Content-Length , Last-Modified and ETag headers.
text: File responses will include appropriate Content-Length , Last-Modified and ETag headers.

short_excerpt: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import FileResponse some_file_path = "large-video-file.mp4" app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/" ) async def main (): return FileResponse ( some_file_path )
text: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import FileResponse some_file_path = "large-video-file.mp4" app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/" ) async def main (): return FileResponse ( some_file_path )

short_excerpt: You can also use the response_class parameter:
text: You can also use the response_class parameter:

short_excerpt: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import FileResponse some_file_path = "large-video-file.mp4" app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/" , response_class = FileResponse ) async def main (): return some_file_path
text: from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import FileResponse some_file_path = "large-video-file.mp4" app = FastAPI () @app . get ( "/" , response_class = FileResponse ) async def main (): return some_file_path

short_excerpt: In this case, you can return the file path directly from your path operation function.
text: In this case, you can return the file path directly from your path operation function.

short_excerpt: Custom response class ¶
text: Custom response class ¶

short_excerpt: You can create your own custom response class, inheriting from Response and using it.
text: You can create your own custom response class, inheriting from Response and using it.

short_excerpt: For example, let's say that you want to use orjson with some settings.
text: For example, let's say that you want to use orjson with some settings.

short_excerpt: orjson
text: orjson

short_excerpt: Let's say you want it to return indented and formatted JSON, so you want to use the orjson option orjson.OPT_INDENT_2 .
text: Let's say you want it to return indented and formatted JSON, so you want to use the orjson option orjson.OPT_INDENT_2 .

short_excerpt: You could create a CustomORJSONResponse . The main thing you have to do is create a Response.render(content) method that returns the content as bytes :
text: You could create a CustomORJSONResponse . The main thing you have to do is create a Response.render(content) method that returns the content as bytes :

short_excerpt: from typing import Any import orjson from fastapi import FastAPI , Response app = FastAPI () class CustomORJSONResponse ( Response ): media_type = "application/json" def render ( self , content : Any ) -> bytes : assert orjson is not None , "orjson must be installed" return orjson . dumps ( content , option = orjson . OPT_INDENT_2 ) @app . get ( "/" , response_class = CustomORJSONResponse ) async def main (): return { "message" : "Hello World" }
text: from typing import Any import orjson from fastapi import FastAPI , Response app = FastAPI () class CustomORJSONResponse ( Response ): media_type = "application/json" def render ( self , content : Any ) -> bytes : assert orjson is not None , "orjson must be installed" return orjson . dumps ( content , option = orjson . OPT_INDENT_2 ) @app . get ( "/" , response_class = CustomORJSONResponse ) async def main (): return { "message" : "Hello World" }

short_excerpt: Now instead of returning:
text: Now instead of returning:

short_excerpt: { "message" : "Hello World" }
text: { "message" : "Hello World" }

short_excerpt: ...this response will return:
text: ...this response will return:

short_excerpt: Of course, you will probably find much better ways to take advantage of this than formatting JSON. 😉
text: Of course, you will probably find much better ways to take advantage of this than formatting JSON. 😉

short_excerpt: orjson or Response Model ¶
text: orjson or Response Model ¶

short_excerpt: If what you are looking for is performance, you are probably better off using a Response Model than an orjson response.
text: If what you are looking for is performance, you are probably better off using a Response Model than an orjson response.

short_excerpt: With a response model, FastAPI will use Pydantic to serialize the data to JSON, without using intermediate steps, like converting it with jsonable_encoder , which would happen in any other case.
text: With a response model, FastAPI will use Pydantic to serialize the data to JSON, without using intermediate steps, like converting it with jsonable_encoder , which would happen in any other case.

short_excerpt: And under the hood, Pydantic uses the same underlying Rust mechanisms as orjson to serialize to JSON, so you will already get the best performance with a response model.
text: And under the hood, Pydantic uses the same underlying Rust mechanisms as orjson to serialize to JSON, so you will already get the best performance with a response model.

short_excerpt: Default response class ¶
text: Default response class ¶

short_excerpt: When creating a FastAPI class instance or an APIRouter you can specify which response class to use by default.
text: When creating a FastAPI class instance or an APIRouter you can specify which response class to use by default.

short_excerpt: The parameter that defines this is default_response_class .
text: The parameter that defines this is default_response_class .

short_excerpt: In the example below, FastAPI will use HTMLResponse by default, in all path operations , instead of JSON.
text: In the example below, FastAPI will use HTMLResponse by default, in all path operations , instead of JSON.

short_excerpt:
from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI ( default_response_class = HTMLResponse ) @app . get ( "/items/" ) async def read_items (): return "

Items

This is a list of items.

"
text:
from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI ( default_response_class = HTMLResponse ) @app . get ( "/items/" ) async def read_items (): return "

Items

This is a list of items.

"

short_excerpt: You can still override response_class in path operations as before.
text: You can still override response_class in path operations as before.

short_excerpt: Additional documentation ¶
text: Additional documentation ¶

short_excerpt: You can also declare the media type and many other details in OpenAPI using responses : Additional Responses in OpenAPI .
text: You can also declare the media type and many other details in OpenAPI using responses : Additional Responses in OpenAPI .
